metadata.title: Subduction Zones Beneath Indonesia Imaged by Phase Velocity Tomography
metadata.study_area: Indonesian archipelago — Sunda-Java Trench, Banda Arc, Molucca Sea, Celebes Sea
metadata.method: Weighted joint inversion of Rayleigh wave tomography (387 earthquakes, 31 stations, 20-143 s period, 2009-2013) and ambient noise tomography (30 stations, 2009-2010, 8-77 s); 3D Vs model resolved 0-330 km depth (50-250 km best resolution)
conclusions: Sunda-Java Trench: systematic along-strike W→E transition from continuous intact slab (>300 km in west near Wharton Fossil Ridge) to increasingly disrupted/thickened structures; slab torn at ~100 km depth near Sunda-Java boundary; deep seismic gap beneath central Java (200-450 km); slab thickens to ~200 km near Java-Timor junction — reflects older subducting lithosphere and NNE-directed subduction dynamics promoting mantle resistance and buckling

Banda Arc: Timor and Seram slab segments merge into single 'table-knife' structure below ~250 km depth — supports single-slab rollback model (Spakman & Hall 2010); Timor segment = seismogenic core (northward subduction), Seram = quieter northern limb with vertical tearing/delamination (Bird's Head underthrusting); W-directed hinge-retreat-dominated system

Molucca Sea (Profile A7B7): prominent ~50 km thick shallow LVZ centered at ~50 km depth, interpreted as intensely deformed serpentinized mantle wedge and fragmented crust trapped between converging slabs; asymmetric inverted-U HVA geometry — thicker/higher velocity on eastern side (Halmahera), thinner/gentler dip on western side (Sangihe); highest velocity at ~200 km depth beneath eastern subduction; western subduction shows seismicity to greater focal depths
Profile A6B6 (Celebes + western Molucca): HVA of Celebes slab from ~100-250 km depth, thickening below 150 km; Celebes and western Molucca seismicity trends converge at ~150 km depth
Eastern Borneo: persistent LVZ -6% to -8% at 40 km depth, extends to 210 km (decaying above 150 km)
Northern Borneo: multilayered lithosphere (HVA at 40-70 km → LV layer at ~90 km → HVA below ~190 km) implies complex interplay — fossil slab, compositional stratification, or vertical buckling

properties.name: Molucca Sea Collision Zone — Shallow LVZ (~50 km depth)
properties.interpretation: Prominent ~50 km thick low-velocity zone (LVZ) centered at ~50 km depth beneath the Molucca Sea arc-arc collision zone, resolved in Profile A7B7. Accompanied by intense shallow seismicity; thickens toward both ends of the profile. Interpreted as a zone of intensely deformed, serpentinized mantle wedge material and fragmented crust trapped and heated between the two converging Sangihe and Halmahera slabs. The Molucca Sea plate is caught in an advanced stage of collapse between two converging island arcs — consistent with the terminal collisional phase predicted by numerical models of divergent double subduction (Zhang et al. 2017). Feature not resolved in previous global body-wave models.

properties.name: Molucca Sea — Asymmetric Double-Sided Subduction HVA
properties.interpretation: Asymmetric inverted-U high-velocity anomaly (HVA) in Profile A7B7 characterizes the double-sided Molucca Sea subduction. Eastern side (Halmahera slab) is thicker with more abundant seismicity at intermediate depths and highest velocity anomaly at ~200 km depth. Western side (Sangihe slab) has thinner anomaly with gentler dip but seismic activity extending to greater focal depths. Zhang et al. (2017) attributed this asymmetry to earlier and faster initiation of subduction on the western side. Model depth limit (~330 km) precludes imaging the full lower interface of the slab.
properties.sangihe_slab_character: thinner, gentler dip, seismicity extends to greater depth
properties.halmahera_slab_character: thicker, larger velocity anomaly, highest velocity at ~200 km depth, more abundant seismicity at intermediate depths

properties.name: Celebes Sea Slab + Western Molucca Slab — Profile A6B6
properties.interpretation: Profile A6B6 traverses both the Celebes Sea subduction zone and the western branch of the Molucca Sea subduction. Two distinct seismic trends correlate with the Celebes slab and western Molucca slab, converging at ~150 km depth and merging into near-vertical distribution at greater depths. The dominant HVA follows the Celebes slab downdip direction, persists from ~100 to 250 km depth, with noticeable thickening below 150 km. The S-wave model at this location predominantly resolves a unified, pronounced HVA whose morphology aligns more definitively with the Celebes Sea slab rather than the Molucca slab.

properties.name: Banda Arc Slab — 'Table-Knife' Single-Slab Rollback Structure
properties.interpretation: High-resolution tomography resolves the 3D Banda Arc slab structure using seven cross-sections (Figure 13). Timor segment (northward subduction, high seismicity 100-200 km) and Seram segment (southward subduction, reduced seismicity, Bird's Head underthrusting) appear as two separate structures at shallow depths. At the 131°E longitudinal profile, both segments fully merge below ~250 km depth into a single 'table-knife'-shaped coherent structure. This deep continuity provides key constraint supporting the single-slab rollback model of Spakman & Hall (2010) rather than a double-slab interpretation. The slab's dramatic ~180° curvature records its response to increasing mantle resistance during rapid southward rollback. Timor segment = actively subducting seismogenic core; Seram segment = northern limb with vertical tearing, delamination, or thermal erosion. Maximum velocity anomaly near ~130°E.

properties.name: Western Sunda Trench Slab — Continuous to >300 km (Profile A1B1)
properties.interpretation: Near the Wharton Fossil Ridge (western Sunda Trench), Profile A1B1 images the most clearly defined slab in the study, subducting to over 300 km depth as a continuous intact HVA. Hypocenters <100 km align with the anomaly's upper interface; deeper earthquakes locate within the HVA. Subducting lithosphere here is ~40 Ma old. This is the simplest/most intact slab in the study, consistent with relatively young oceanic lithosphere and the NE-directed subduction kinematic regime where slab geometry is well-coupled.

properties.name: Eastern Borneo — Persistent Low-Velocity Anomaly (40-210 km)
properties.interpretation: Coherent low-velocity anomaly beneath eastern Borneo visible at 40 km in both RW and AN models. Anomaly is -6% to -8% at 40 km depth, extends to 210 km (amplitude decreasing above 150 km). Spatially extensive — tectonically confined by surrounding subduction systems. At 90 km depth, peak negative perturbation reaches -12% (3.51 km/s) beneath NW Borneo. Origin not fully explained; may reflect relict thermal anomaly or magmatic underplating associated with the Borneo block's rotational history and its role as a stable Sundaland continental block flanked by active subduction.
